mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive survey of Western philosophy from pre-Socratic inquiry through Hellenistic schools (A.1.c), medieval Scholasticism (A.2), Renaissance humanism (A.3.a), early modern rationalism and empiricism (A.3.b), Kantian critical philosophy (A.3.c.i), 19th-century idealism (A.3.d.i), positivism (A.3.d.ii), and 20th-century analytic and continental traditions (A.3.e).

Directly surveys the development of systematic rational inquiry across Western traditions from Presocratic cosmology through modern philosophy (A), and non-Western traditions including Indian, Chinese, Japanese, and Islamic philosophy (B).

Aristotle's works on logic, metaphysics (substance, potentiality, actuality), biology, ethics (Nicomachean Ethics), and politics, his empiricist methodology and rejection of Platonic Forms, and his influence on medieval Scholasticism and Islamic philosophy directly map to the philosophy of Aristotle (A.1.b.iii).

Socrates' ethical concerns, the Socratic method of dialectical questioning, and his foundational influence on Plato and all subsequent Western philosophy directly map to the ethical concerns and positions of Socrates (A.1.b.i).

Aquinas' synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology in the Summa Theologiae, including natural theology, proofs for God's existence, and faith-reason reconciliation, directly represents the age of the Schoolmen and Thomas Aquinas' synthesis (A.2.c).

Philosophy's core branches (epistemology, ethics, logic, metaphysics) and major traditions (Western, Arabic-Persian, Indian, Chinese) map broadly across the history of Western philosophy (A) and non-Western philosophy (B).

The Islamic Golden Age's philosophical production, bridging Greek antiquity and European Renaissance through the translation and synthesis of Aristotelian thought, directly represents the contribution of Arabic and Jewish philosophy and the role of Islamic philosophers in increasing the influence of Aristotle in the West (A.2.b).

Eastern philosophy encompassing Indian systems (Vedanta, Samkhya, Nyaya, Buddhism, Jainism) maps to Indian philosophy (B.1), Chinese systems (Confucianism, Taoism, Legalism, Mohism) to Chinese philosophy (B.2), and Japanese traditions (Zen, Shinto) to Japanese philosophy (B.3).

The Enlightenment covering Descartes, Locke, Hume, and Kant, political theory of natural rights, and rational reform directly maps to philosophy in the period of the Enlightenment (A.3.c), including epistemological issues (A.3.c.i), philosophy of science (A.3.c.ii), and social and political philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Plato's Theory of Forms, his dialogues on politics, ethics, metaphysics, and epistemology, and his Academy directly map to the philosophy of Plato (A.1.b.ii).

Cicero's adaptation of Hellenistic philosophical schools (Academic Skepticism, Stoicism, Epicureanism) into Latin and his philosophical vocabulary connect to Hellenistic and Roman philosophy (A.1.c), spanning multiple schools.

Kant's transcendental idealism, categorical imperative, and critical examination of reason directly represent Kant's critical examination of reason within Enlightenment epistemological issues (A.3.c.i). His synthesis of empiricism and rationalism and influence on German idealism connects to A.3.d.i.
Laozi as the traditional author of the Tao Te Ching, articulating the Dao and wu wei, directly connects to the classical Chinese philosophical school of Taoism (B.2.a).

Shankara's systematization of Advaita Vedanta, asserting the identity of Atman with Brahman through commentaries on the Upanishads and Brahma Sutras, directly maps to the schools of Vedanta and the contribution of Sankara (B.1.d).
Ancient Greek civilization as the origin of Western philosophy through Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, and Hellenistic schools directly maps to ancient Greek and Roman philosophy (A.1).

The Bhagavad Gita's synthesis of dharma, karma yoga, jnana yoga, and bhakti yoga, with Vedantic non-dualism and Vaishnavism, connects to early Indian philosophical thought and Hindu sacred literature (B.1.a), the schools of Vedanta through commentaries by Shankara and Ramanuja (B.1.d), and Hindu philosophy (C.1).

Descartes' method of radical doubt, mind-body dualism, and foundationalism directly represent developments in the Rationalist tradition, specifically the antiempirical character of Descartes' metaphysics and dualism (A.3.b.ii).

Marx's historical materialism and dialectical method directly represent the dialectical Materialism of Marx and Engels within the 19th-century empirical and scientific tradition (A.3.d.ii), and Marxist thought continues as a 20th-century philosophical development (A.3.e.ii).

Galileo's experimental method, mathematization of nature, and championing of heliocentrism connect to the philosophy of nature and the mathematically ordered character ascribed to the natural world, including the influence of discoveries in physics and astronomy on philosophy (A.3.a.iii).

The Scientific Revolution's departure from Aristotelian frameworks through heliocentrism, empiricism, and mechanical philosophy connects to the philosophy of nature (A.3.a.iii) and the rise of Empiricism and Rationalism (A.3.b).

Machiavelli's empirical analysis of power, The Prince, and Discourses on Livy directly represent political theory in the Renaissance, the views of Machiavelli on the nature and moral status of political power (A.3.a.i).

Avicenna's Peripatetic philosophy derived from Aristotle, his Book of Healing covering logic, metaphysics, and natural science, and his influence on medieval Islamic theology and European Scholasticism directly represent the contribution of Arabic philosophy and the role of Islamic philosophers (A.2.b).

Locke's tabula rasa and experiential origin of ideas represent developments in the Empiricist tradition (A.3.b.i). His inquiry into the origin and nature of reason connects to Enlightenment epistemology (A.3.c.i), and his social contract theory and natural rights to the concern with freedom and equality of citizens (A.3.c.iii).

Dante's Divine Comedy within a Thomistic-Scholastic framework and Aristotelian cosmology connects to the age of the Schoolmen and the attempt to reconcile philosophy and theology (A.2.c), and to the Scholastic method through its theological exposition (A.2.d).

Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection fundamentally influenced philosophical thought, connecting to developments in the philosophy of science and the effect of scientific discoveries on philosophical thought (A.3.c.ii).

The history of science tracing empirical inquiry connects to the philosophy of nature and the influence of discoveries in astronomy and physics on philosophy (A.3.a.iii) and to developments in the philosophy of science (A.3.c.ii).

The history of mathematics from Pythagorean number theory through Greek axiomatic proof connects to the metaphysics of number and Pythagorean speculations about number and the nature of reality (A.1.a.iii).
The Tang dynasty's Confucian-Buddhist-Daoist religious synthesis connects to Neo-Taoist and Buddhist thought in Chinese philosophy (B.2.b).

The Renaissance's humanism, recovery of classical antiquity, and empirical observation directly connect to philosophy in the Renaissance, including humanism and the influence of Plato on moral theory (A.3.a.ii).

Romanticism's German Idealism and reaction against Enlightenment rationalism connect to the resurgence of the metaphysical spirit and the Idealism of Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel (A.3.d.i) and the reaction against Rationalism through Schopenhauer and Kierkegaard (A.3.d.iii).

The cognitive capacity for reasoning and the tensions between reason and faith connect to the rise of problems in the theory of knowledge (A.1.a.ii) and Enlightenment epistemological issues including Kant's critical examination of reason (A.3.c.i).

Voltaire's advocacy for civil liberties, religious toleration, deism, and his critique of institutional religion connect to social and political philosophy in the Enlightenment and developments in religious philosophy (A.3.c.iii).

Mahayana Buddhism's Madhyamaka emptiness philosophy, Yogacara consciousness doctrine, and bodhisattva ideal connect to early Indian philosophical thought and Buddhist sacred literature (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).

Theravada Buddhism's Pali canonical basis, Abhidhamma philosophy, and meditation practices connect to early Indian philosophical thought and Buddhist writings on selflessness and Nirvana (B.1.a) and Buddhist philosophy (C.2).

Liberalism's intellectual lineage from Locke through Montesquieu and J.S. Mill, centering individual rights, liberty, and consent of the governed, connects to social and political philosophy in the Enlightenment concerning the freedom and equality of citizens (A.3.c.iii).

Alexander's conquests triggering the Hellenistic period and the spread of Greek philosophy across the Near East and Central Asia connect to Hellenistic and Roman philosophy, developments from the time of Alexander III the Great (A.1.c).
Confucius' teachings on personal morality, filial piety, and governmental virtue directly represent the classical Chinese school of Confucianism (B.2.a) and connect to Confucian philosophy (C.3).
Rumi's Sufi doctrine of divine love and annihilation of the ego, grounded in Hanafi Islamic jurisprudence, connects to Islamic philosophy associated with religion (C.6).

The moral dichotomy of good and evil spanning philosophical and religious traditions, with Abrahamic and Manichaean cosmic dualism, connects to Jewish and Christian philosophy during the Hellenistic Age and attempts to relate philosophical schools to theology (A.1.c.v).

Democracy originating in Athens and evolving through Enlightenment social contract theory connects to social and political philosophy in the Enlightenment concerning the freedom and equality of citizens (A.3.c.iii).

Ethics as moral philosophy covering consequentialism, deontology, and virtue ethics connects to the ethical concerns of Socrates (A.1.b.i) and J.S. Mill's theory of knowledge and ethics within the 19th-century empirical tradition (A.3.d.ii).
Ibn Khaldun's systematic empirical social analysis and historiographical method, distinct from medieval Islamic theology, connect to the contribution of Arabic and Jewish philosophy (A.2.b).
